gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to improve prediction of functional effects of sequence variants (distinguishing effect vs. neutral) over the previous SNAP method. The problem is motivated by the need to interpret genetic variation for personalized health. Key constraints include limited and imbalanced training data, sequence similarity between proteins, and the difficulty of annotating neutral variants. The initial hypothesis is that evolutionary information is most informative, but additional features can improve predictions. The method is designed to predict molecular function change, not disease association.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: SNAP2 is a neural network classifier that improves over state-of-the-art in distinguishing effect from neutral variants. It achieves 83% two-state accuracy on >100k variants, outperforms other methods and their combinations. Evolutionary information is strongest signal, but SNAP2 also performs well without alignments (SNAP2_noali) for sequence orphans. Reliability index helps select high-confidence predictions.
process_signals: Improved performance from screening many features and refining datasets
SNAP2 outperforms SNAP and other methods
Performance varies by organism
Reliability index calibrated for experimental follow-up
Alignment-free version developed for orphans
metrics: Accuracy for top half of effect predictions: >96%

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: The problem is distinguishing functional from neutral variants. Existing methods focus on disease-causing variants, but SNAP2 predicts molecular function change. Challenges include lack of good neutral annotations and performance drop for proteins without informative alignments. SNAP2 improves over SNAP in performance, alignment-free predictions, and runtime.
process_signals: Focus on molecular function change, not disease
Avoid distinguishing gain-of-function from loss-of-function
Motivation: many organisms have 10-20% orphans
SNAP2 improves over SNAP in three aspects
failures_or_limits: Performance decreases for proteins without alignments

section_gists.heading: Data sets
section_gists.gist: Training data from PMD (effect/neutral), EC (neutral from enzyme alignments), disease variants (OMIM, HumVar) as effect. Additional test sets: LacI and HIV-1 protease. Evaluation sets: ALL (101,515 variants), PMD_HUMAN, PMD_NON-HUMAN. Data imbalance: PMD has 74% effect; EC used to balance neutrals.
process_signals: PMD annotations: 'no change' = neutral, any change = effect
EC method: enzymes with same EC number and >40% identity yield neutral variants
Disease variants classified as effect, may overestimate functional changes
LacI and HIV-1 protease used as independent test sets
Data split into ALL, PMD_HUMAN, PMD_NON-HUMAN
failures_or_limits: Disease variants may not all be functional changes

section_gists.heading: Cross-validation
section_gists.gist: Proteins clustered by sequence similarity (PSI-BLAST, E<10^-3, single linkage). Clusters randomly split into 10 subsets for cross-validation: 8 training, 1 cross-training, 1 testing. Ensures no similar sequences between sets. Performance reported only on test sets.
process_signals: Clustering to avoid overfitting due to sequence similarity
Imbalance in subset sizes accepted
Tenfold cross-validation with rotation
failures_or_limits: Subset sizes varied due to cluster sizes

section_gists.heading: Prediction method
section_gists.gist: Neural networks chosen over SVMs, decision trees, random forests due to runtime efficiency. Two output units: neutral and effect. Networks trained with cross-training to optimize parameters. Final prediction uses average of 10 networks, output difference ranges -100 to +100.
process_signals: SVMs and neural networks performed similarly; neural networks chosen for efficiency
Ten networks trained; all used for final prediction to avoid over-training
Output difference used as prediction score

section_gists.heading: Input features
section_gists.gist: Features include biophysical properties, sequence, PSIC profiles, secondary structure, solvent accessibility, flexibility, SWISS-PROT annotations. New features: AAindex properties, binding residues, disorder, terminal proximity, contact potentials, co-evolution, Pfam/PROSITE annotations, low-complexity regions, global composition. Sliding windows up to 21 residues.
process_signals: Original SNAP features retained
New features added to improve prediction
Sliding window approach for local context

section_gists.heading: Feature selection
section_gists.gist: Greedy bottom-up feature selection per network, using AUC on cross-training set. Balanced training, repeated until no improvement. Combined features across networks, then backward elimination. Final architecture optimized (hidden nodes, learning rate, momentum). Tested on held-out sets to check overfitting.
process_signals: Feature selection done separately for each network
Avoided local maxima by repeated training and recording best step
Backward elimination to remove non-contributing features
No significant overfitting observed

section_gists.heading: Predicting effects without alignments
section_gists.gist: SNAP2_noali developed by excluding evolutionary information and using global features, amino acid indices, and a substitution matrix of effect probabilities from SNAP2 predictions. Used for proteins without alignments; users notified of reduced accuracy.
process_signals: Developed to handle orphans (10-20% of proteins)
Used SNAP2 predictions to create effect probability matrix
Implementation selects SNAP2 or SNAP2_noali based on available information

section_gists.heading: Performance measures
section_gists.gist: Metrics: accuracy/precision, coverage/recall for effect and neutral, F-measure, two-state accuracy (Q2), MCC. Standard deviation/error from 1000 bootstrap sets (50% sampling without replacement). Reliability index (RI) from normalized output difference (0-10).
process_signals: Bootstrap without replacement for error estimates
RI used to assess prediction confidence
metrics: Accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, Q2, MCC, RI

section_gists.heading: SNAP2 significantly improves predictions
section_gists.gist: SNAP2 outperforms SNAP, PolyPhen-2, SIFT on ALL dataset (AUC 0.905). Performance gain from new features and better data. Comparisons complicated by training overlaps and threshold optimization. SNAP2 results are cross-validated; others may be overestimated.
process_signals: SNAP2 AUC: 0.905 vs SNAP 0.880, PolyPhen-2 0.853, SIFT 0.838
Improvement from novel features and more/better data
ROC curves significantly different (P<10^-4)
Other methods' performance may be overestimated due to training overlap

section_gists.heading: Performance differed between the human and non-human PMD data
section_gists.gist: SNAP2 performs slightly better on non-human PMD. On human PMD, PolyPhen-2 on par with SNAP2; SIFT best for neutrals. On non-human, SNAP2 outperforms others. PolyPhen-2 likely overestimated due to training overlap.

PolyPhen-2 performance on human may be overestimated
Neutral prediction harder across methods
failures_or_limits: PolyPhen-2 training overlap with human PMD

section_gists.heading: Blind method combinations might be worse than a good single method
section_gists.gist: Combining SIFT and PolyPhen-2 did not improve over SNAP2 alone. For neutral variants, combination slightly improved over individual methods, but SNAP2 still better. Meta-methods may improve over simple combinations.
process_signals: Combining methods by agreement did not beat SNAP2
SNAP2 alone outperformed SIFT+PolyPhen-2 combination
Meta-methods might be better but not tested here
failures_or_limits: Simple combination strategy may be suboptimal

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from SNAP to SNAP2 by incorporating more features and refining datasets, leading to significant performance gains.
Evolutionary information is the most important feature, but alignment-free predictions are needed for orphan proteins.
Neutral variants are inherently harder to predict due to data imbalance and annotation challenges.
Combining methods naively does not guarantee improvement; a well-optimized single method can outperform combinations.
Performance varies across organisms, possibly due to training data biases.

events.summary: The paper addresses the challenge of distinguishing functional from neutral sequence variants for personalized health.
SNAP2 is introduced as a neural network classifier that improves over previous methods by screening many features and refining datasets.
Cross-validation on >100k variants shows SNAP2 achieves 83% two-state accuracy.
Evolutionary information from alignments is the strongest signal, but SNAP2 also performs well without alignments.
SNAP2_noali developed for sequence orphans, using global features and a substitution matrix from SNAP2 predictions.
SNAP2 predicts molecular function change, not disease association, avoiding gain/loss-of-function distinctions.
Training data compiled from PMD, EC, disease variants, and protein-specific studies, with careful class assignment.

evidence_bank.quote: Elucidating the effects of naturally occurring genetic variation is one of the major challenges for personalized health and personalized medicine.

canonical_events.summary: Need to distinguish functional from neutral sequence variants for personalized health; existing methods focus on disease, not molecular function change.
SNAP2 designed as neural network classifier to improve over SNAP by screening many features and refining datasets; predicts molecular function change, not disease.
Training data compiled from PMD, EC, disease variants (OMIM, HumVar), and protein-specific studies (LacI, HIV-1 protease); careful class assignment (effect/neutral).

evidence.quote_or_span: Elucidating the effects of naturally occurring genetic variation is one of the major challenges for personalized health and personalized medicine.

In contrast, the current version of our SNAP (Screening for Non-Acceptable Polymorphisms) method, SNAP2, does not predict the variant effect as “disease or not” but rather as “change of molecular function or not”.

Here, we introduce SNAP2, a novel neural network based classifier that improves over the state-of-the-art in distinguishing between effect and neutral variants. Our method's improved performance results from screening many potentially relevant protein features and from refining our development data sets.
